# Catalysts for electrochemical CO2 conversion: material sustainability perspective

[*npj Materials Sustainability*](/npjmatsustain)
**volume 3**, Article number: 22 (2025)
[Cite this article](#citeas)

1902 Accesses

1 Citations

1 Altmetric

[Metrics details](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9/metrics)

### Subjects

## Abstract

The electrochemical reduction of CO2 (eCO2R) presents a promising pathway for addressing climate change by converting CO2 into value-added chemicals and fuels. A crucial aspect of this technology is the choice of catalyst materials, which directly influences the selectivity, stability and sustainability of the process. Here we introduce a streamlined supply risk assessment coupled with life-cycle environmental impact associated with various catalysts used in eCO2R for products, including formate, carbon monoxide, ethylene, and ethanol to provide a well-rounded perspective for catalysts’ sustainability assessment. We compare more than 68 case studies in eCO2R using various metal-based catalysts. Our results show that Bi-based catalysts for formate production have the highest supply risk and environmental burdens, while Sn-based catalysts show overall better durability and much lower sustainability concerns. Copper-based catalysts’ supply risk for ethylene conversion is lower and more concentrated, whereas the supply risk for ethanol conversion is more dispersed. Our findings further confirm that improving catalyst performance—especially the stability—can substantially mitigate both supply risks and environmental impacts. This highlights the urgent need for standardized methodologies to assess catalyst stability and novel strategies to further improve catalyst stability using both material and system approaches. We call for stronger cross-sector collaboration to further integrate criticality and sustainability assessment frameworks with more granular datasets and dynamic spatial and temporal representation, for continuous eco-design improvement of eCO2R catalysts.

## Introduction

The manufacturing industry is heavily dependent on fossil fuels, both as the primary energy source for high-pressure, high-temperature thermochemical reactions and as key reactants. The CO2 emissions arise not only from fuel combustion but also as by-products of chemical reactions[1](#ref-CR1 "Bui, J. C. et al. Continuum Modeling of Porous Electrodes for Electrochemical Synthesis. Chem. Rev. 122, 11022–11084 (2022)."),[2](#ref-CR2 "Andrew, R. M. Global CO2 emissions from cement production (2024)."),[3](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#ref-CR3 "Ober, J. A. Mineral commodity summaries 2017. 202 (Reston, VA, 2017)."). In 2022, industry sectors contributed up to nearly 38% of global fuel combustion emissions alone, which was approximately 12.9 gigaton of CO2[4](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#ref-CR4 "Greenhouse Gas Emissions from Energy Data Explorer. 
                  https://www.iea.org/data-and-statistics/data-tools/greenhouse-gas-emissions-from-energy-data-explorer
                  
                 (2024)."). These factors make reducing CO2 emissions in the manufacturing industry particularly challenging.

The electrochemical CO2 reduction reaction (eCO2R) offers a promising route to produce low-carbon-emission chemicals using CO2, water, and renewable electricity (Fig. [1a](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#Fig1)). When CO2 is captured directly from the air, eCO2R has the potential to close the carbon loop for fuels and chemicals that release CO2 during their end use. Research has shown that ethylene and ethanol are particularly appealing in efforts to reduce carbon intensity without compromising market value, as they combine high product value with significant carbon intensity (ton of equivalent CO2 per ton of product)[5](#ref-CR5 "O’Brien, C. P. et al. CO2 Electrolyzers. Chem. Rev. 124, 3648–3693 (2024)."),[6](#ref-CR6 "Nabil, S. K. et al. Comparative life cycle assessment of electrochemical upgrading of CO2 to fuels and feedstocks. Green Chem. 23, 867–880 (2021)."),[7](#ref-CR7 "Roh, K. et al. Early-stage evaluation of emerging CO2 utilization technologies at low technology readiness levels. Green Chem. 22, 3842–3859 (2020)."),[8](#ref-CR8 "Kibria, M. G. et al. Electrochemical CO2 Reduction into Chemical Feedstocks: From Mechanistic Electrocatalysis Models to System Design. Adv. Mater. 31, 1807166 (2019)."),[9](#ref-CR9 "Lark, T. J. et al. Environmental outcomes of the US Renewable Fuel Standard. PNAS 119, e2101084119 (2022)."),[10](#ref-CR10 "Sternberg, A. et al. Life cycle assessment of CO2-based C1-chemicals. Green Chem. 19, 2244–2259 (2017)."),[11](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#ref-CR11 "Artz, J. et al. Sustainable Conversion of Carbon Dioxide: An Integrated Review of Catalysis and Life Cycle Assessment. Chem. Rev. 118, 434–504 (2017).").

A., Crane, J. & Dinh, C.-T. Comparative analysis of electrolyzers for electrochemical carbon dioxide conversion. Catal. Today 423 (2023).").

Am. Chem. Soc. 143, 6855–6864 (2021)."). In some cases, gold can also serve as a catalyst for CO production[54](#ref-CR54 "Shang, H. et al. Efficiency and selectivity of CO2 reduction to CO on gold gas diffusion electrodes in acidic media. Nat. Commun. 12, 4943 (2021)."),[56](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#ref-CR56 "Kortlever, R. et al. Palladium–gold catalyst for the electrochemical reduction of CO2 to C1–C5 hydrocarbons. Chem. Commun. 52, 10229–10232 (2016)."). Other studies employed aluminum[57](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#ref-CR57 "Zhong, M. et al. Accelerated discovery of CO2 electrocatalysts using active machine learning. Nature 581, 178–183 (2020).") or gallium[30](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#ref-CR30 "Bagchi, D. et al. Structure-Tailored Surface Oxide on Cu-Ga Intermetallics Enhances CO2 Reduction Selectivity to Methanol at Ultralow Potential. Adv. Mater. 34, e2109426 (2022).") for the production of ethylene or methanol from CO2. The formation of C2+ products such as ethylene, ethanol, and propanol from CO reduction often uses copper-based catalysts with significant copper content[58](#ref-CR58 "Ripatti, D. S., Veltman, T. R. & Kanan, M. W. Carbon Monoxide Gas Diffusion Electrolysis that Produces Concentrated C2 Products with High Single-Pass Conversion. Joule 3, 240–256 (2019)."),[59](#ref-CR59 "Li, J. et al. Constraining CO coverage on copper promotes high-efficiency ethylene electroproduction. Nat. Catal. 2, 1124–1131 (2019)."),[60](#ref-CR60 "Zhuang, T.-T. et al. Copper nanocavities confine intermediates for efficient electrosynthesis of C3 alcohol fuels from carbon monoxide. Nat. Catal. 1, 946–951 (2018)."),[61](#ref-CR61 "Pang, Y. et al. Efficient electrocatalytic conversion of carbon monoxide to propanol using fragmented copper. Nat. Catal. 2, 251–258 (2019)."),[62](#ref-CR62 "Wang, X. et al. Efficient electrosynthesis of n-propanol from carbon monoxide using a Ag–Ru–Cu catalyst. Nat. Energy 7, 170–176 (2022)."),[63](#ref-CR63 "Wang, X. et al. Efficient upgrading of CO to C3 fuel using asymmetric C-C coupling active sites. Nat. Commun. 10, 5186 (2019)."),[64](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#ref-CR64 "Ozden, A. et al. Energy- and carbon-efficient CO2/CO electrolysis to multicarbon products via asymmetric ion migration–adsorption. Nat. Energy 8, 179–190 (2023)."). In recent years, several attempts have been made to scale the CO2 or CO electrolyzer up by two orders of magnitude in size[65](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#ref-CR65 "Crandall, B. S. et al. Kilowatt-scale tandem CO2 electrolysis for enhanced acetate and ethylene production. Nat. Chem. Eng. 1, 421–429 (2024)."),[66](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#ref-CR66 "Nelson, V. E. et al. Scaling CO2 Electrolyzer Cell Area from Bench to Pilot. ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces 16, 50818–50825 (2024)."). These studies have suggested that catalysts such as copper- or silver-based, with proven performance, are of particular interest for the large-scale deployment of this conversion technology.

Metal-based catalysts are the primary choice for eCO2R due to their high activity and selectivity, but scaling this technology under decarbonization goals introduces material supply risks. Converting 1 ton of CO2 daily would require 170 g of Cu, meaning over 90 tons annually for recycling 1 Gt of CO2[67](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#ref-CR67 "Belsa, B. et al. Materials challenges on the path to gigatonne CO2 electrolysis. Nat. Rev. Mater. 9, 535–549 (2024)."). As demand for critical metals like Cu, Ag, and Sn grows, the system becomes increasingly vulnerable to price volatility, supply chain disruptions, and geopolitical risks, all of which could undermine its economic viability and hinder large-scale deployment. These supply challenges are compounded by the environmental burdens associated with metal extraction, highlighting the importance of understanding their life-cycle sustainability. However, despite the widespread use of metal-based catalysts in eCO2R, little is known about their supply risks or the environmental burdens linked to the preference for metal selection. Here we introduce a streamlined supply risk assessment coupled with life-cycle environmental impact associated with various catalysts used in eCO2R for products like formate, carbon monoxide, ethylene, and ethanol to provide a well-rounded perspective for catalysts’ sustainability assessment.

## Material Requirements for Catalysts in Electrochemical CO2 Conversion

### Direct conversion

The direct conversion of CO2 takes place in a single reactor and converts CO2 into desirable products (Fig. [2a](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#Fig2)), such as ethanol, ethylene, and formate[68](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#ref-CR68 "Gao, G., Obasanjo, C. A., Crane, J. & Dinh, C.-T. Comparative analysis of electrolyzers for electrochemical carbon dioxide conversion. Catal. Today 423 (2023)."). Numerous reactor configurations exist, with the most promising for scale-up being the zero-gap membrane electrode assembly[69](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#ref-CR69 "Ma, W. et al. Electrocatalytic reduction of CO2 and CO to multi-carbon compounds over Cu-based catalysts. Chem. Soc. Rev. 50, 12897–12914 (2021).") (Fig. [1b](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#Fig1)). Many different types of metals have been employed as catalysts for this reaction. The most promising metal for valuable C2+ products is copper, possibly due to its favourable adsorption energies for both \*CO and \*H[70](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#ref-CR70 "Nitopi, S. et al. Progress and Perspectives of Electrochemical CO2 Reduction on Copper in Aqueous Electrolyte. Chem. Rev. 119, 7610–7672 (2019)."). Metals that benefit the production of other products, such as formate, have also been determined[70](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#ref-CR70 "Nitopi, S. et al. Progress and Perspectives of Electrochemical CO2 Reduction on Copper in Aqueous Electrolyte. Chem. Rev. 119, 7610–7672 (2019)."). Polymer binders are often incorporated into the catalyst layer, which is deposited onto a support, to bind the catalyst to the support and to tune the activity and the selectivity of the reaction[71](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#ref-CR71 "Pham, T. H. M. et al. Enhanced Electrocatalytic CO2 Reduction to C2+ Products by Adjusting the Local Reaction Environment with Polymer Binders. Adv. Energy Mater. 12 (2022).").

![figure 2](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs44296-025-00065-9/MediaObjects/44296_2025_65_Fig2_HTML.png)

**a** Diagram illustrating the One-step and Two-step conversion methods. **b**–**d** Turnover frequency (TOF) of various catalyst metals for the production of carbon monoxide, C2 products (ethylene in blue, ethanol in pink), and formate from CO2, respectively. **e** TOF of copper catalyst for ethylene production from carbon monoxide.

When employing an anion exchange membrane, these reactors provide a basic cathodic environment which favours eCO2R[72](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#ref-CR72 "Salvatore, D. A. et al. Designing anion exchange membranes for CO2 electrolysers. Nat. Energy 6, 339–348 (2021)."). However, this high pH environment also leads to the formation of (bi)carbonates that can cross through the membrane and cause the loss of available reactant and increase of downstream separation costs[73](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#ref-CR73 "Lees, E. W. et al. Exploring CO2 reduction and crossover in membrane electrode assemblies. Nat. Chem. Eng. 1, 340–353 (2024)."). Acidic systems may offer a solution to this issue, but they suffer from their own problems, such as an acidic catholyte environment that favours hydrogen evolution reaction over eCO2R[74](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#ref-CR74 "Habibzadeh, F. et al. Ion Exchange Membranes in Electrochemical CO2 Reduction Processes. Electrochem. Energy Rev. 6 (2023).").

To be economically viable, these electrolyzers require operation at an industrial scale for tens of thousands of hours[8](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#ref-CR8 "Kibria, M. G. et al. Electrochemical CO2 Reduction into Chemical Feedstocks: From Mechanistic Electrocatalysis Models to System Design. Adv. Mater. 31, 1807166 (2019)."). However, reported stability performance currently lags and will need to be improved to ensure industrial feasibility. As examples of high product selectivity in the MEA cell configuration, studies have shown Faradaic Efficiencies (FEs) of 52% for ethanol[26](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#ref-CR26 "Wang, X. et al. Efficient electrically powered CO2-to-ethanol via suppression of deoxygenation. Nat. Energy 5, 478–486 (2020)."), 92.8% for ethylene[22](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#ref-CR22 "Merino-Garcia, I., Albo, J. & Irabien, A. Tailoring gas-phase CO2 electroreduction selectivity to hydrocarbons at Cu nanoparticles. Nanotechnology 29, 014001 (2018)."), 82% for formate[40](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#ref-CR40 "Li, L. et al. Stable, active CO2 reduction to formate via redox-modulated stabilization of active sites. Nat. Commun. 12, 5223 (2021)."), and 87% for formic acid[31](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#ref-CR31 "Fan, L. et al. Electrochemical CO2 reduction to high-concentration pure formic acid solutions in an all-solid-state reactor. Nat. Commun. 11, 3633 (2020)."). Some higher FEs have been reported but in configurations less suited to scale-up, such as 91% for ethanol[29](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#ref-CR29 "Xu, H. et al. Highly selective electrocatalytic CO2 reduction to ethanol by metallic clusters dynamically formed from atomically dispersed copper. Nat. Energy 5, 623–632 (2020).") and 95% for formate[40](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#ref-CR40 "Li, L. et al. Stable, active CO2 reduction to formate via redox-modulated stabilization of active sites. Nat. Commun. 12, 5223 (2021)."). A summary of different studies across multiple different electrolyzer configurations is shown in Table [S1](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#MOESM1).

### Two-step conversion

The reduction of CO2 generates multiple products, which are carbon monoxide, ethylene, ethanol, formate, acetate, methane and propanol. Many of these products share the same reaction pathway[8](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#ref-CR8 "Kibria, M. G. et al. Electrochemical CO2 Reduction into Chemical Feedstocks: From Mechanistic Electrocatalysis Models to System Design. Adv. Mater. 31, 1807166 (2019)."), among these, carbon monoxide is special because it is the common intermediate for the formation of most C2+ products during electrolysis[75](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#ref-CR75 "Yoshio Hori, Ryutaro Takahashi, Yuzuru Yoshinami, a. & Murata, A. Electrochemical Reduction of CO at a Copper Electrode. J. Phys. Chem. B (1997)."),[76](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#ref-CR76 "Hori, Y., Murata, A., Takahashi, R. & Suzuki, S. Electroreduction of carbon monoxide to methane and ethylene at a copper electrode in aqueous solutions at ambient temperature and pressure. J. Am. Chem. Soc. (2002).") (Fig. [2a](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#Fig2)).

In an eCO2R process using an anion exchange membrane, an alkaline environment on the cathode side is ideal. However, a reaction between CO2 and the hydroxide ions formed during the process produces bicarbonate, resulting in reactant loss. This issue can be avoided with electrochemical CO reduction (eCOR), as CO does not react with hydroxide ions. This allows highly alkaline conditions to be maintained without reactant depletion, offering a key advantage for this method.

Despite this, a technical challenge with eCOR is the low solubility of CO in aqueous electrolyte solutions. To overcome this, the catalyst is designed to enable fast CO transport to the catalyst layer, allowing for current densities higher than 200 mA·cm−2 to be achieved[5](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#ref-CR5 "O’Brien, C. P. et al. CO2 Electrolyzers. Chem. Rev. 124, 3648–3693 (2024)."). Most eCOR studies at high current densities use gas diffusion electrodes with Nafion and other perfluorosulfonic acid ionomers. These materials have hydrophobic backbones that extend the three-phase (gas, liquid, and solid) boundary into the catalyst layer, enhancing the overall transport of CO to the active sites and maintaining high current density throughout the reaction[59](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#ref-CR59 "Li, J. et al. Constraining CO coverage on copper promotes high-efficiency ethylene electroproduction. Nat. Catal. 2, 1124–1131 (2019)."),[64](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#ref-CR64 "Ozden, A. et al. Energy- and carbon-efficient CO2/CO electrolysis to multicarbon products via asymmetric ion migration–adsorption. Nat. Energy 8, 179–190 (2023)."),[65](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#ref-CR65 "Crandall, B. S. et al. Kilowatt-scale tandem CO2 electrolysis for enhanced acetate and ethylene production. Nat. Chem. Eng. 1, 421–429 (2024)."),[77](#ref-CR77 "Li, H. et al. CO electrolysis to multicarbon products over grain boundary-rich Cu nanoparticles in membrane electrode assembly electrolyzers. Nat. Commun. 15, 4603 (2024)."),[78](#ref-CR78 "Zhu, P. et al. Direct and continuous generation of pure acetic acid solutions via electrocatalytic carbon monoxide reduction. PNAS 118 (2021)."),[79](#ref-CR79 "Li, J. et al. Enhanced multi-carbon alcohol electroproduction from CO via modulated hydrogen adsorption. Nat. Commun. 11, 3685 (2020)."),[80](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#ref-CR80 "Ji, Y. et al. Selective CO-to-acetate electroreduction via intermediate adsorption tuning on ordered Cu–Pd sites. Nat. Catal. 5, 251–258 (2022).").

### Material requirements for catalyst

Overall, the choice of catalyst material and its loading depends on the targeted product, with copper (Cu) being predominant for hydrocarbons and alcohols, tin (Sn) and bismuth (Bi) for formate, and silver (Ag) for carbon monoxide production. For the direct production of ethylene and methane from CO2, copper (Cu)-based catalysts are most commonly applied, with loadings typically ranging from 0.25 to 1.25 mg·cm−2 [12](#ref-CR12 "K, X. et al. Bipolar membrane electrolyzers enable high single-pass CO2 electroreduction to multicarbon products. Nat. Commun. 13, 3609 (2022)."),[13](#ref-CR13 "Nguyen, T. N. et al. Catalyst Regeneration via Chemical Oxidation Enables Long-Term Electrochemical Carbon Dioxide Reduction. J. Am. Chem. Soc. 144, 13254–13265 (2022)."),[14](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#ref-CR14 "Dinh, C.-T. et al. CO2 electroreduction to ethylene via hydroxide-mediated copper catalysis at an abrupt interface. Science 360, 783–787 (2018)."),[16](#ref-CR16 "Ozden, A. et al. High-Rate and Efficient Ethylene Electrosynthesis Using a Catalyst/Promoter/Transport Layer. ACS Energy Lett. 5, 2811–2818 (2020)."),[17](#ref-CR17 "Choi, C. et al. Highly active and stable stepped Cu surface for enhanced electrochemical CO2 reduction to C2H4. Na

### Abstract

Electrochemical reduction of CO2 to produce chemicals or fuels may contribute to the zero-emission goal of the chemical industry. Here, we report the state-of-the-art and future perspective of electrochemical CO2 conversion processes to produce CO, syngas, formic acid and ethylene. We selected and explored six routes: low-temperature CO production, low-temperature formic acid production, low-temperature ethylene production, high-temperature CO production, high-temperature syngas production, and a tandem approach to produce ethylene. For these routes, we describe the current level of development, performance indicators, and costs. The state-of-the-art of the chlor-alkali process is included as an example of a commercially applied electrochemical process. We calculate the economic performance of the various pathways in terms of levelized production costs and we use a learning curve method to project costs up to 2050. The greenhouse gas performance for all routes is determined and compared to the current reference of production from fossil-based resources. We conclude that high-temperature solid-oxide electrolysis to produce CO and syngas is the most developed and closest to reaching break-even levelized production cost in comparison to the fossil reference. Low-temperature electrolysis processes are at a lower technology readiness level and still need a substantial reduction in investment costs and improvements in process efficiency to achieve break-even with incumbent technology. The most promising of the low-temperature processes is formic acid production. Electrochemical production of formic acid, CO, and syngas results or can soon result in substantial GHG savings compared to their fossil-based alternatives. The extent to which savings can be achieved depends merely on the carbon intensity of the local power grid, or more generally, the supplied electricity. Electrochemical CO2 conversion to produce ethylene would require a very low emission factor of electricity (<50 gCO2 per kW h) to be competitive with current production methods and is therefore not likely to contribute significantly to the zero-emission goal of the petrochemical industry in the foreseeable future. Research gaps are identified at various levels: improvement of the performance of the various components, such as catalysts and electrodes, and of purification of feedstock and product streams. Pilot and demonstration projects of the entire value chain from the CO2 stream to the final product are needed to more accurately determine the performance, total investment costs, and operating and maintenance costs in an industrial environment.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Electrochemical methods utilize electricity to convert CO₂ into valuable chemicals, showcasing high selectivity and efficiency, vital for sustainable chemical production.
Optimizing catalysts, especially copper-based ones, enhances stability and selectivity in CO₂ reduction, addressing supply risks and environmental impacts associated with metal usage.
Integrating CO₂ conversion processes with renewable energy sources maximizes sustainability, reducing reliance on fossil fuels and enabling low-carbon chemical production.
